qtype: Standard form conversion, percentage calculation of area
tex:
(a) The Earth has a surface area of approximately 510 100 000 km$^2$.
\newline

    (i) (b) Write this surface area in standard form.
\newline

        

(c) Water covers 70.8\% of the Earth's surface.
\newline

        Work out the area of the Earth's surface covered by water.
\newline

        

(d) The table shows the surface area of some countries and their estimated population in 2017.
\newline

    \begin{tabular}{|l|r|r|}
        \hline
        Country & Surface area (km$^2$) & Estimated population in 2017 \\
        \hline
        Brunei & $5.77 \times 10^3$ & 433 100 \\
        China & $9.60 \times 10^6$ & 1 388 000 000 \\
        France & $6.41 \times 10^5$ & 67 000 000 \\
        Maldives & $3.00 \times 10^2$ & 374 600 \\
        \hline
    \end{tabular}

    (i) (e) Find the total surface area of Brunei and the Maldives.
\newline

        

(f) The ratio surface area of the Maldives : surface area of China
        can be written in the form 1 : n.
\newline

        Find the value of n.
\newline

        n = 

(g) Find the surface area of France as a percentage of the surface area of China.
\newline

        

(h) Find the population density of the Maldives.
\newline
        [Population density = population $\div$ surface area]

        

(i) The population of the Earth in 2017 was estimated to be $7.53 \times 10^9$.
\newline

    The population of the Earth in 2000 was estimated to be $6.02 \times 10^9$.
\newline

    (i) (j) Work out the percentage increase in the Earth's estimated population from 2000 to 2017.
\newline

        

(k) Assume that the population of the Earth increased exponentially by y\% each year for these
        17 years.
\newline

        Find the value of y.
\newline

        y =

        France & $6.41 \times 10^5$ & 67 000 000 \\

        \hline
    \end{tabular}

        [Population density = population $\div$ surface area]

tex:

(d) the interquartile range.

\newline

tex:
(a) P   O   S   S   I   B   I   L   I   T   Y
    Morgan picks two of these letters, at random, without replacement.
\newline

    (i) (b) Find the probability that he picks

        the letter Y first,

        

(c) the letter B then the letter Y,

        

(d) two letters that are the same.
\newline

        

(e) Morgan now picks a third letter at random.
\newline

    (i) (f) Find the probability that

        all three letters are the same,

        

(g) exactly two of the three letters are the same,

        

(h) all three letters are different.
\newline

content.content:
Find the probability that he picks

        the letter Y first,
the letter B then the letter Y,

two letters that are the same.
\newline

Morgan now picks a third letter at random.
\newline

Find the probability that

        all three letters are the same,
exactly two of the three letters are the same,

all three letters are different.
\newline
